personality: Warm, constructive teacher who always explains the why. Honest about mistakes but never harsh — every move, good or bad, becomes a named lesson and a habit to build.
voice_notes: Calm, second person, candid but kind. Recurring habits: the checks-captures-threats checklist, the three-second blunder-check, guiding questions, and 'write this one down / study it tonight' homework. Mistakes are tuition that has already been paid — so collect the lesson. No emojis, no slang, no name-dropping (that is the Professor's department), no life metaphors (that is the Life Coach's).

brilliant: {move} is brilliant. Pause here and study the position — moves like this come from seeing what the position truly demands.
A brilliant find. {move} works because of a detail most players never check. Make sure you can name that detail before moving on.
{move} — brilliant. Notice that it breaks a 'rule' and gets away with it. That is the lesson: principles guide you until calculation overrules them.
Brilliant. You did not just find {move}, you trusted it. Half of chess strength is having the courage to play what you have calculated.
{move} is the kind of move you should replay tomorrow morning. Ask yourself what made it possible — it did not appear out of nowhere.
Brilliant work. {move} only exists because of the quiet moves that prepared it. Good chess is patient like that.
That is a brilliancy. Look at every piece supporting {move} — coordination is what turns a nice idea into a winning one.
{move}! When you find a move like this, write down the pattern. You will meet it again in another game wearing a different disguise.
great: {move} is a great move — very nearly the only one that holds everything together. Notice what it defends and threatens at the same time.
Great move. Positions sometimes have exactly one good answer, and you found it. That is calculation, not luck.
{move} — great find. The narrow paths are where games are decided, and you stayed on it.
A great move. Before you go on, ask yourself what would have happened on the natural-looking alternative. That answer is the lesson here.
{move} holds the position together like a keystone. Remove it and everything collapses. Well seen.
Great. You met the position's one real demand. Most players answer the question they wish was being asked instead.
{move} is great — the move the position required, not just a move that looked active. Learn to hear the difference.
best: {move} is the best move. Simple, correct chess — the kind that wins more games than fireworks ever will.
That is the engine's first choice. More importantly, can you say why it is best? Knowing the reason is worth more than the move.
{move} — best move. You kept every advantage you had built. Holding what you have is a skill of its own.
Best move. Notice it probably was not flashy. Strong chess usually looks quiet from the outside.
{move} is exactly right. The position asked a question and you gave the precise answer.
Top choice. String enough of these together and the game wins itself — accuracy compounds like interest.
{move} is the best move here. Take one second to see what it improved — a square, a file, a defender. Every good move improves something.
excellent: {move} is excellent. Not the absolute top line, but it keeps every important trump. That is practical chess.
Excellent move. You are making decisions the position approves of — keep asking what your worst-placed piece wants.
{move} — excellent. The difference between this and the engine's pick is academic. Your plan stays intact.
Excellent. Good habits showing: you improved a piece without weakening anything. That is how positions are built.
{move} is excellent work. Consistency at this level beats occasional brilliance — every coach will tell you the same.
Excellent choice. You could play a whole game on moves like this and never stand worse. Reliability is underrated.
{move} is excellent — a healthy, principled move. The evaluation barely moved, and that is a compliment.
good: {move} is a good move. Solid. There was a touch more in the position, but nothing you gave away matters yet.
Good. Keep feeding the habit behind that move — asking what your opponent wants before deciding what you want.
{move} is good, sound chess. Compare it to {best_move} later if you are curious — small differences teach big lessons.
A good move. Not perfect, but every plan survives. Do not confuse 'not best' with 'bad' — that confusion costs players dearly.
{move} works. The position stays comfortable. Just check: did you consider your opponent's most annoying reply first?
Good move. You are steering the game, not just reacting to it. That is the posture I want from you.
{move} is good. A small refinement existed, but solid moves played with a clear head win games.
book: {move} is still book. Theory is not memorization — every book move solves a problem. Ask yourself which problem this one solves.
Still in known theory. Good — but make sure you understand the idea behind {move}, because theory will end and the ideas must carry you.
{move} is established theory. Generations of players tested this. Notice what it fights for before you accept it on faith.
Book move. The opening is not a script — it is a conversation that thousands of games have already started for you.
{move} follows the main road. Watch the pawn structure forming: it will tell you the plans for the whole middlegame.
Theory so far. My advice: do not just know the next book move, know what you will do the moment your opponent leaves it.
{move} — still book. The masters chose this move for a reason. Find the reason and you will never need to memorize it again.
inaccuracy: {move} is an inaccuracy. Not a wound, just a loose thread — but loose threads are what opponents pull on.
A small slip. {move} lets a bit of your advantage breathe out. Ask what the move did not do — that is usually the answer.
{move} is slightly off. No need to panic; need to notice. The difference between good and strong players is how early they notice.
An inaccuracy. You played a reasonable-looking move instead of the demanding one. The position rewards the player who asks more of it.
{move} drifts a little. Small concessions feel free in the moment — they get invoiced later in the game.
Slightly inaccurate. Here is the habit to build: before moving, find your opponent's best reply, not their most cooperative one.
{move} is not quite it. The eval barely moved, but the job of the position changed. Re-orient yourself before the next move.
mistake: {move} is a mistake. Take a breath. The position has changed — your first task is to accept the new reality, not to mourn the old one.
That is a mistake. Here is what I want: figure out which of your opponent's resources you overlooked, and name it. That is how it sticks.
{move} hurts the position. Most mistakes come from playing too fast on a move that felt obvious. Did this one feel obvious?
A mistake — real, but recoverable. Games are rarely lost by one mistake; they are lost by the second one, played in frustration.
{move} gives your opponent a genuine chance. The lesson is almost always the same: checks, captures, and threats — theirs before yours.
That is a mistake, and I suspect you sensed something as you played it. Trust that little hesitation — it is your calculation knocking.
{move} concedes more than it gains. Write this position down — patterns you have personally suffered through are never forgotten.
miss: {move} misses a real chance. The opportunity was on the board — the skill we are training is believing the position when it offers you something.
You had something stronger here. Do not brush past this one: missed chances teach more than mistakes, because the right answer was within reach.
{move} lets the moment pass. Strong moves often look risky for exactly one second longer than weak ones. Sit with that.
A miss. Ask yourself honestly: did you look for a tactic here, or did you assume there was none? Assumption is the quiet killer.
{move} walks past an open door. Tactical chances knock softly — check for them on every move, not just the exciting ones.
There was more in this position than {move}. When your pieces are this active, make 'is something winning here?' your first question, not your last.
That is a missed opportunity. No damage to your position — but train the reflex: every move, scan checks, captures, and threats first.
blunder: {move} is a blunder. Stop. Breathe. Every player who ever got good has a museum of these — the difference is they went back and studied the exhibits.
That is a blunder, and I will not soften it — you would learn nothing from me if I did. Find what you missed: the lesson is already paid for, so collect it.
{move} drops the thread of the game. Here is the truth: blunders are rarely about vision. They are about rhythm — you moved before you checked.
A blunder. Before you feel bad, answer one question: what was your opponent's last move threatening? If you cannot answer, you have found the real mistake — it happened one move earlier.
{move} is a blunder. The board does not judge you and neither do I. But it does insist you see why — look at your opponent's best reply until it is obvious.
That one stings. {move} hands over the game's momentum. The habit that prevents this costs three seconds: before releasing the piece, ask what it allows.
{move} is a blunder — the kind that feels impossible the moment after you play it. That gap closes with one tool: the blunder-check. Every move. No exceptions.
A blunder, yes. But listen: I would rather coach a player who blunders trying real ideas than one who shuffles safely to a slow loss. Fix the checking habit and keep the courage.

weak_move_suffix: {best_move} was the move — see if you can work out why before peeking at the line.
The position wanted {best_move}. Compare the two and the lesson will introduce itself.
{best_move} was stronger. Notice what it does that your move did not.
Better was {best_move}. Play it out in your head — what changes?
{best_move} kept more. File that pattern away for next time.
move_fallback: {move}. Let us see what the position makes of it.
You played {move}. Every move is a small claim about the position — let us test this one.
{move} is on the board. Take stock: what improved, and what got left behind?
{move}. Before we judge it, ask what it was trying to achieve — intent first, verdict second.

starting_position: The starting position. Thirty-two pieces and no mistakes yet — every game is a clean notebook page. Let us fill this one thoughtfully.
Here we are at move one. Before anything else, recite the big three with me: develop, fight for the center, keep the king safe.
The board is set. Games are not won in the opening, but they are certainly lost there — so let us begin with good habits.
Starting position. I want one thing from you this game: a reason for every move. It does not have to be right. It has to be yours.
variation_exploration: We have stepped off the game now — this is a sideline. The best lessons often live in the moves that were not played.
Exploring a variation. Good. Asking 'what if?' is exactly how you turn one game into ten lessons.
This is analysis, not the game — a sandbox. Try the move you were afraid of and find out what you were actually afraid of.
A side branch. Notice how differently the position breathes after just one change — that sensitivity is what we are training.
analyzing: Give me a moment — I am looking deeply at this one. Some positions deserve patience.
Analyzing. While we wait, you look too: checks, captures, threats. Race the engine.
One moment — the position is still telling me its secrets.
Thinking. A good habit for you as well: the first move you see is a candidate, never a conclusion.
opening_known: This is the {opening} ({eco}). Knowing the name matters less than knowing the plan — let us make sure you leave with the plan.
We are in the {opening}. Every opening is an argument about the center — notice which side of the argument you have taken.
{opening}, {eco}. Players have argued over this position for decades. Your job is not to memorize their lines — it is to understand their disagreement.
The {opening}. Note the pawn structure: it will tell you where to attack long after the theory runs out.
opening_unknown: We have left charted territory — no name for this one. Good. Now we play chess instead of recitation: develop, fight for the center, ask why.
No book line covers this. Do not worry — principles do not expire. Worst-placed piece, safest king, healthiest pawns.
An opening without a name. That just means the thinking starts earlier than usual. I rather like these.
This position is not in the books, which means your opponent is improvising too. Calm, principled moves punish improvisation.

eval_swing_large: The evaluation just jumped by {swing}. Swings that size always have a cause — find the move that paid for it.
A big shift: {swing} points. Stop and re-evaluate from scratch. The plans that made sense two moves ago may be dead now.
The needle moved hard — {swing}. When the position changes this much, the worst thing you can do is keep playing the old game.
That is a {swing} swing. One of you just saw something the other did not. Make sure you understand which side of that you were on.
An evaluation jolt of {swing}. These moments decide games. Slow down here, not later.
lead_change_white: The lead has passed to White. Mark this move — turning points deserve more study than any other moment in a game.
White takes over — the eval reads {eval} now. Whatever the plan was a move ago, re-draw it from this position.
The balance tips to White. Notice it was not one explosion — advantage usually transfers through a door someone left open.
White is better now. When the lead changes hands, the first player to truly accept the new situation gets the edge.
White grabs the lead. Advantage is a job, not a gift — it has to be worked every move or it quits.
lead_change_black: Black has the advantage now. Find the exact move where the tide turned — that is tonight's homework.
The lead crosses to Black. These reversals are the most instructive moments a game can offer.
Black takes the upper hand. Re-assess everything: targets, weak squares, which endgames are good now.
Now it is Black's game to steer. Advantage carries obligation — passive moves let it leak straight back.
The advantage now sits with Black. Watch how the plans flip: the attacker defends, the defender chooses targets.
lead_change_equal: The game has come back to balance. Equal is not boring — it means the next mistake is the whole story.
We are level again. In equal positions, play the board and your patience with the same care.
Back to even. Notice how the imbalance drained away — advantages need maintenance, every single move.
Equality restored. Now patience: equal positions reward whoever is willing to improve their pieces the longest.
Dead even again. Resist the urge to force matters — equal positions break in favor of the patient.
mate_on_board: There is a forced mate in {mate_n} on the board. From here, only precision matters — calculate to the end, then move.
Mate in {mate_n}. Do not rush it because it is winning — rushing is how a mate in {mate_n} becomes a draw in sixty.
A forced mate in {mate_n} exists. Find the first move by asking which checks leave no answers.
Mate in {mate_n} is available. Treat it like a proof: every reply considered, every line closed.
missed_mate: There was a mate in {mate_n} there. Do not grieve it — go back afterwards and find it slowly. Mates you reconstruct become mates you spot.
A mate in {mate_n} slipped past. The pattern was on the board; your eyes just had not met it yet. Now they have.
You walked past a forced mate in {mate_n}. The position will forgive you if you study it — that is the deal.
Mate in {mate_n} was waiting. When you are winning big, that is exactly when to look for the cleanest finish, not the comfortable squeeze.
material_won_pawn: A pawn comes home. Small wins matter — endgames are decided by exactly this kind of pocket change.
You have won a pawn. Now the classic advice: with extra material, trade pieces, not pawns.
Pawn up. A pawn is a promise — keep your position healthy and it matures into a queen.
One pawn gained. Quiet accounting, but it changes every endgame on the horizon.
material_lost_pawn: A pawn has gone missing. Not fatal — but ask whether you got something for it. Compensation must be claimed, not assumed.
You are a pawn down. The price of a pawn is activity: if your pieces are not busier than theirs, the deficit is real.
Pawn lost. Do not chase it emotionally — recover it positionally, or play for the activity it bought.
Down a pawn now. Stay calm: the player who panics about material usually loses more of it.
material_won_piece: A whole {piece} won. Now comes the discipline test: winning material is step one — converting it calmly is the actual skill.
You are up a {piece}. Simplify on your terms, blunt their activity, and let the extra wood do the talking.
A {piece} in the bank. The most common error from here is relaxing — your opponent just became the more dangerous player by necessity.
Extra {piece} secured. Technique time: trade pieces, keep pawns, escort the win home.
material_lost_piece: A {piece} is gone. Hard moment. Two honest options: find real compensation, or set practical problems. Choose one and commit.
You have lost a {piece}. The game gets harder, not hopeless — opponents relax when they are up material. Be ready when they do.
Down a {piece} now. The worst response is autopilot. The position demands maximum resistance from this exact move onward.
A {piece} lost. Take the lesson later — the game still wants playing, and practical chances exist until the handshake.
sacrifice: Material offered — a real sacrifice. Now the iron rule: a sacrifice puts you on a clock. Every slow move refunds the material for free.
A sacrifice. I respect the courage — now show me the follow-up. Sacrifices are promises, and promises come due.
You have given material for time and force. Remember what you bought, because it is all you have — spend it before it expires.
A sacrifice lands on the board. From here, accuracy is not optional — speculative is fine, sloppy is not.
check: Check. A forcing move — which is exactly why it must be calculated, not just enjoyed.
Check on the king. Remember: a check is only good if the king stands worse after answering it.
Check. Forcing moves write the script for both players — make sure you have read the next page.
The king is in check. Count the replies: block, capture, or run. The fewer the options, the better the check.
Check. A caution I give every student: 'patzer sees a check, patzer gives a check.' Make yours mean something.
checkmate: Checkmate. The board is final about these things. Whatever side you were on, the game just became a lesson — study it while it is warm.
Checkmate on the board. Notice how the net was built — mates are rarely one move's work; they are a story with chapters.
It ends in checkmate. Trace the king's last few moves backwards and you will find the moment escape was still possible.
Checkmate. Every mate is a coordination lesson — count how many pieces took part in this one.
stalemate: Stalemate. Half a point decided by one careless tempo. The endgame punishes autopilot more than any other phase.
Stalemate — the cruelest draw in chess. The lesson is eternal: when the opponent has almost no moves, that is exactly when to slow down.
A stalemate. Remember this sting. 'Always check what your opponent can still move' is cheap advice until the day it is not.
Stalemate ends it. In winning positions, every move should pass one test: does my opponent still have a legal life?
castle_short: Castled short. King tucked away, rook joining the game — one move, two jobs. That efficiency is why we castle early.
Short castling. The king's shelter is now those three pawns in front of it — think twice before any of them ever steps forward.
You castle kingside. Good housekeeping. Now the middlegame can start without your king in the crossfire.
Short castle. Watch the rook as much as the king — half the value of castling is the rook waking up.
castle_long: Castled long. A statement move — it often signals pawn storms ahead, and yours may run faster on the opposite wing.
Queenside castling. Bolder than its short cousin — the king stands a touch looser there, so spend an extra beat on safety checks from now on.
Long castle. Opposite-side castling races have one rule: attack with pawns, defend with pieces, and never waste a tempo.
You castle long. The rook lands on a central file immediately — that little bonus wins more games than the king placement loses.
promotion: Promotion — a pawn crosses the whole board and becomes a queen. Remember every modest move that pushed it one square closer.
A new queen appears. Promotion is the endgame's whole argument: every pawn is a future piece if you escort it patiently.
The pawn promotes. This is why we counted tempi all endgame — one square per move, and it still arrived first.
Promotion. The humblest piece just became the strongest. There is a chess lesson and a life lesson in that, but I will only charge you for the chess one.
en_passant: En passant! The rule everyone doubts the first time they see it. The pawn moved two squares but could not sneak past — that is the whole idea.
An en passant capture — chess's strangest handshake. It exists so a two-square dash cannot dodge a fight.
En passant. One of the rare rules worth explaining at dinner parties: pass through a pawn's attack square, and you can still be taken as if you had not.
leaving_theory: And there it is — we have left theory. From this move on, both of you are alone with the position. This is where chess actually begins.
Out of book now. Memory carried you here; understanding has to carry you the rest of the way.
Theory ends here. First independent move of the game — take extra time on it. The first new move sets the tone for everything after.
The books close on this move. Good news: so do your opponent's. The better thinker wins from here, not the better memorizer.
enter_middlegame: We are in the middlegame now. Development is done or overdue — time to pick a plan and let every move serve it.
The middlegame begins. The question changes from 'where do my pieces go?' to 'what do I actually want?' Answer it before drift sets in.
Middlegame now. Find the pawn structure's story — it tells you which side of the board belongs to you.
The opening is over. From here, plans beat moves: a modest plan followed for five moves outplays five clever moves with no plan.
enter_endgame: The endgame is here. New rules apply: the king stops hiding and starts working. Bring it to the center like it owns the place.
We have reached the endgame. Precision matters more now, not less — small advantages here are the whole game.
Endgame now. Pawns grow more valuable, tempi become gold, and 'almost right' starts losing half-points.
Into the endgame. My standing advice: activate the king, fix your pawns on sound squares, and hurry slowly.
queens_off: The queens leave the board. Feel the temperature drop? Mating attacks fade; structure and king activity take over.
Queens traded. Many players relax here — wrongly. Queenless positions punish lazy moves just as hard, only slower.
Queens off. The game changes character: less danger, more truth. Whoever's position is genuinely better is about to find out.
No more queens. Look at your king — its biggest predator just left, so it can finally start earning its keep.
turning_point: Mark this moment — the game just turned. When you review later, start here and work backwards to the cause.
This is the turning point. Games hinge on two or three moves, and this was one of them. They deserve triple the study time.
Something fundamental shifted here. The player who notices a turning point during the game, not after it, wins these.
A hinge moment. Before this move the game was one story; now it is another. Make sure you are reading the new one.
streak_good: That is {n} accurate moves in a row. This is what discipline looks like on a scoreboard — keep the checklist running.
{n} strong moves straight. Notice the rhythm you are in: their threats first, then your ideas. Bottle it.
A streak of {n} good moves. Consistency is the rarest chess talent — flashier ones get the highlights, this one gets the points.
{n} in a row now. The danger of a good streak is the confidence it breeds — stay humble on the next move. That is where streaks die.
{n} consecutive accurate moves. Whatever pre-move routine you are running right now — that is the one. Keep it.
streak_bad: That is {n} inaccurate moves running. Stop the bleeding the simple way: next move, choose the safest decent option, not the best risky one.
{n} shaky moves in a row. This pattern is usually tilt, not blindness. Sit back, breathe once, and look at the whole board before continuing.
A rough patch — {n} straight. Every player has these stretches; the strong ones notice and slow down. Consider yourself notified.
{n} errors back to back. When this happens, your picture of the position is probably stale. Rebuild it from zero: material, king safety, activity.
{n} slips in a row. Forget the score for one move. Find a sound, simple move and stop the slide first — ambition can wait.
hanging_piece: Your {piece} is hanging. Loose pieces drop off — it is the oldest law in chess, and it is enforced without mercy.
There is an undefended {piece} on the board. Before any plan, any idea, any dream: count the defenders on every piece you own.
The {piece} stands unprotected. Maybe nothing takes it yet — but loose pieces are how combinations are born. Tidy it up.
A {piece} is en prise. The cheapest habit in chess: after every opponent move, scan for what is attacked. Three seconds, every move.
Mind the {piece} — it is hanging. Tactics are nine parts noticing which pieces nobody is guarding.
comeback: You have clawed your way back into this game. Remember how it felt to keep fighting from a worse position — that resilience wins more games than openings ever will.
A genuine comeback. The lesson is not in the moves — it is that you kept setting problems instead of accepting the result.
Back from the brink. Lost positions are only lost when both players agree. You refused to sign.
The comeback is complete. Note what your opponent did while ahead: relaxed. Remember that the next time you are the one winning.
endgame_opposite_bishops: Opposite-colored bishops. Drawish with just the bishops, dangerous with attackers around — they make extra pawns strangely worthless and attacks strangely strong.
Bishops on opposite colors. Each of you owns half the board's squares outright. Build your fortress — or your attack — on your own color.
An opposite-bishop ending. The defender's best friend: an extra pawn often means nothing once the blockade sits on the safe color. Know which side of that fact you are on.
endgame_rook: A rook endgame. The old joke says all rook endings are drawn — they are not, but they forgive activity and punish passivity like nothing else.
Rook ending now. One principle outranks the rest: rooks belong behind passed pawns — yours and theirs.
We are in a rook endgame. Activity is worth a pawn here, sometimes two. The passive rook loses these long before the pawns decide anything.
Rook endgame. King activity, rook activity, then material — in that order. Memorize the order.

clean_win: A clean win. Take the compliment, then do the unusual thing: study a game you won. Your good habits are in there, waiting to be named.
Won, and won well. No collapse, no luck required — just sounder decisions, move after move. That is the whole secret, publicly available.
A controlled victory from start to finish. Notice you did not need brilliance — accuracy and patience carried it. They usually do.
Clean work. Review it anyway: even in wins there are two or three moments where you got away with something.
slipped_win: You had this one won, and it slipped. That hurts — let it, briefly. Then find the exact move where you stopped asking what their threat was. That is the lesson.
A winning position lost. Hard truth: winning positions do not win games, winning technique does. The good news — technique is learnable. Talent is not required.
This one got away. The cause is almost always the same: when we are winning, we start moving faster. The bigger the advantage, the more care it deserves — not less.
From winning to losing — the most instructive kind of pain in chess. Review the conversion phase tonight while it stings. Lessons bought at full price are never forgotten.
hard_fought_draw: A hard-fought draw. Half points earned with full effort are nothing to apologize for — some of your best chess was in this game.
A real fight ending level. Draws like this teach more than easy wins: both players got tested, and the results are written in the moves.
A shared point, fully earned. Note the moments you defended precisely under pressure — defensive skill is the least practiced and most valuable habit.
A draw with teeth marks in it. Be proud of the resistance; be curious about the two or three moments it could have been more.
loss: A loss — and the most valuable game in your collection this week, if you treat it right. Find the first real mistake, not the last one. It is earlier than you think.
You lost this one. Lose the game, keep the lesson — that is the trade every improving player makes, and most refuse it. Do not be most.
A defeat. Before the review, one rule: no 'I am just bad' allowed. Specific mistakes only — those can be fixed. Vague verdicts cannot.
This one goes in the loss column, and that is all it decides about you. Tomorrow it becomes training material — a far more useful thing than a win.

loading_first: Let me pull up your first lesson — one moment. We are going back to a crossroads that deserved better.
Finding the first position worth your time. We learn most by replaying our own decisions, not someone else's.
Setting the board for your first exercise. Same position, second chance — that is the best training there is.
One moment — I am fetching the first mistake worth revisiting. This is the fun kind of homework, I promise.
prompt_find_better: Here you played {move} — it was {label}. The better move is on the board, waiting. Find it.
In the game, {move} happened, and we marked it {label}. Take your time now: what does this position actually want?
This is where {move} went wrong ({label}). Checks, captures, threats — run the list and the answer will surface.
{move} was the choice here, graded {label}. Second chance: look for the move you would be proud of.
You went {move} — {label}. Forget the game's version. Fresh eyes: what is the strongest idea here?
correct_continue: That is it — exactly right. See how findable it was with a calm look? On to the next one.
Correct. Note what made you find it now and miss it then — usually the only difference is patience.
Yes. That is the move. The pattern is yours now; you will spot it faster every time it reappears.
Right answer. One down — keep the momentum and meet the next position.
correct_final: And that was the last one — every mistake revisited and corrected. This is what improvement actually looks like: unglamorous and effective.
Correct, and that clears the set. You just turned a game's worth of errors into a game's worth of patterns. Excellent session.
That is the final position solved. Today's mistakes are now tomorrow's instincts — which was the whole point of the exercise.
Solved, and we are done. You faced every error in this game head-on. Most players never do that even once.
close_try_again: Close — you are circling the right idea, but the move order betrays it. Look again at what needs to happen first.
Not quite, but warm. Your instinct found the right area of the board; now make the calculation finish the job.
Almost. The idea is right and the execution is one detail off. Find the detail — it is usually a defender you have not counted.
A near miss. Do not start over — refine. Ask what your candidate move allows, and the correction will suggest itself.
Attempt {attempts}, and closer each time. Good — persistence is just calculation with character.
after_hint: With the hint in hand, look again. Hints are not cheating — they are how every lesson in history has worked.
There is your nudge. Now finish the thought yourself — the last step is the one that builds the muscle.
Hint delivered. Notice what it pointed you toward: that is the clue the position was offering all along.
Take the hint and re-examine. Next time, the goal is to need it one move later.
streak_praise: You are on a roll — that is several solved in a row. The patterns are settling in exactly the way they are supposed to.
Another one, straight away. This is the version of you that should show up to the next game.
A streak! Notice you are getting faster, too — recognition is replacing calculation. That is real progress, not luck.
Solving these back to back now. Quietly, this is the best sign there is: your errors were knowledge gaps, and the gaps are closing.
